text: This is Rada Skujian.
I'm one of the neurosurgeons at the Swedish Neuroscience Institute.
We want to take you into the operating room to see a live resection of a very difficult tumor.

We're going to go through the entire process, which includes getting the patient position, getting imaging, and then show how the tumor is removed and how complex this surgical procedure is and kind of giving you a glimpse of what it's like to be a patient and technically the procedure and the details involved in taking this tumor out.
So after the exposure is performed, you can see here, these are the paraspinal muscles.
And then you can start to see part of the spinous process.
And we're doing what's called a very careful subperiosteal dissection of the paraspinal musculature in order to identify the lamina.
And the thoracic lamina is then carefully and meticulously exposed.
So once we've exposed the lamina, you can see here the spinous process, the transverse process, and the costovertebral joint bilaterally.
Now we use a drill, and I'm going to use a drill to basically do the laminectomy.
So I'm using a drill now, and we're first going to localize this to make sure we're at the right levels.
Pen field number four is then placed into the pedicle, and intraoperative fluoroscopic image is confirming that we're at the right level.
Then using the drill, we then do a wide laminectomy of the thoracic spine and take the lamina off.
Once the laminectomy is done and the lamina is removed, we then bring in the operating microscope.
This allows us to view the contents of the surgical cavity under high magnification.
In addition, we're also able to record this.
So as surgeons, I often go back and review my surgical procedure.
So here I am with Dr. Price.
We're carefully taking more bone off.
and taking off now the facet joint and the pedicle.
This is done again under high magnification.

I've drilled off and you can see here I've drilled off the pedicle on the patient's left side and now we're putting some hemostatic agents because there's lots of epidural veins here and I've drilled off the pedicles
and we waxed them using a patty.
We also put some hemostatic agents and you can see there's a little bit of blood coming from the epidural space.
Normally when we do a laminectomy you can see the spinal cord is not really pulsating and typically it pulsates with every heartbeat and here you can see that there's not.
You can actually feel the tumor
Now we're putting some Surgicel into the pedicles where we drilled them off.
And again, this is another way to achieve some hemostasis.
Basically, it kind of tamponades the epidural space and allows for the bleeding to slowly stop.
Now, what we're going to do next is probably the most interesting part of the case.
We're going to go ahead and use a very sharp knife.
It's an 11 blade to incise the dura.

The dura is the covering the spinal cord, and this is a very important part of the procedure because everything that we've done so far in this procedure, which has been so far about three hours, we've taken the bone off, we've carefully dissected around the spinal cord.
You can see each nerve root that's going.
There's one by my sucker on each side, but you can see we've dissected the left side more than the right side.
So now on the left side, we have complete access to the anterior of the spinal cord.

So now I've switched from the 11 blade to a beaver blade and literally right underneath the dura which is that very thin piece that you can see I'm picking up is the spinal cord and the tumor so we're very carefully opening up the dura and then there's the arachnoid so as soon as you can see there's there's spinal fluid coming out that's from the subarachnoid space and you can see there's tumor and the spinal cord right underneath and now

Probably for the first time in a long time, this patient's spinal cord is starting to get pulsations, which means that now we're starting to create some room and taking the pressure off the spinal cord.
And this is done very carefully under the operating microscope.
And you could see there's some nerve roots that are usually seen in the thoracic spine.
Usually they go quite lateral.
And in this case, they've kind of been pushed backwards because of the location of the tumor.
And now we're taking a little bit more bone off to go more cranial so we can have a little bit more access.
And while we're doing that, we're also letting the spinal fluid to come out.
And this allows us to get the surgical cavity ready for the actual surgery.
Now we're using some what's called pot scissors to open the dura.
And again, you can see that we're just going to tack up the dura here with some

sutures these are 4-0 Neuralon so that's the size of the suture and we're going to carefully tack up the dura here and you can see as we let more spinal fluid out there's more bleeding and so now we're going to put a very gentle piece of it's a patty it's called the Telfa patty laterally to sort of compress the epidural vein so once you start to lose spinal fluid the epidural veins start to bleed more and so now we put
now a total of four TACUP sutures in and we're going to start to carefully open the dura and try to get access to the spinal cord and the tumor.
You can see that there's nerve roots that are being pushed over and I'm carefully trying to use an arachnoid plane and to dissect the cord from the actual tumor.

you can see there's the tumor we're sort of looking at it and then the cord is being pushed off to the right side and there's the nerve root exiting out the neuroframen and i've gone i'm trying to go in between the nerve root and you can see the tumor underneath and i'm trying to get a little bit more control
So there's two branches to a nerve root.
There's the sensory and motor branches.
The sensory branches are coming off posteriorly, and then the motor branches are anterior.
And then in between it, the structure that's coming out, this is called the dentate ligament.
So this structure needs to be identified and cut, which is what we're doing.
So it's not neural tissue.
And when you cut the dentate ligament, which what we're doing here is carefully identifying, and then we're going to divide it.
Now we're able to mobilize the spinal cord.
So we have to go to the next thoracic nerve root, identify the dentate ligament here, and then you can see I'm carefully cutting along the dentate ligament.
Now I'm going to cut the rest of it off.
And once you're able to find and release the dentate ligament both above and below, you can see that the spinal cord is very mobile.
But still, this is an extremely dangerous operation, and you can see how difficult it is.
That tumor is anterior to the cord, and the cord is the structure that's pushed posteriorly.
You can see a little vein.
And now I'm using very careful electrocautery and a knife to get specimen.
That's the tumor itself.
So we're getting some specimen.
I've called our surgical pathologist and I'm taking more and more tissue.
And the reason to get interoperative diagnosis is that so we can know what we're dealing with here.
And so I've taken multiple specimens and the specimens are sent to pathology.
Now I'm using a very interesting instrument.

It's an ultrasonic aspirator and the ultrasonic aspirator is very useful in a case like this because we're able to resect the tumor and this is done carefully under the operating microscope and you can see here we're just carefully removing the tumor and
You can see here, we've carefully identified the planes that need to be identified, and using the ultrasonic aspirator, we're removing the tumor and trying to debulk the tumor.
In these kind of tumors, it's very difficult.
You can't just go in there and try to yank the tumor out.

What you have to do is get both proximal and distal control of the lesion and then just take your time and very carefully and meticulously take as much of this tumor as you can and it takes hours to do a surgery like this.
And you can see you have to be laser focused on what the end goal is here and basically we want to try to get as much of this tumor out
as possible without injuring the spinal cord and having the patient have any more weakness than they already have.
In this case, this tumor ended up being quite vascular.

And so while we're taking the tumor out, we're also very carefully trying to minimize the amount of blood loss and also maintain a very clean surgical cavity and understanding the 3D relationship of the tumor to the actual spinal cord.

and the view that you have with the operating microscope is incredible and actually i have to say just looking at it this way it doesn't really serve it the actual view that we have during surgery is you can see the hairs on you know the patient's back and it's it's just millimeters of accuracy and you can really see like right here in this view i can tell the difference between
tumor tissue versus the actual cord.
And you can see the tumor is very anterior and you'll start to see the cord is now starting to drop back to its normal position after being pushed over to the patient's left side.
And we're carefully dissecting the rest of the tumor that's coming off the dura.
This is taking hours to do.

this procedure and you can see how there's just no room for any mistakes and how we carefully with each move, we're just taking more and more of the tumor out, carefully exposing the rest of the anterior compartment of the spinal cord.
You can actually see the vessels in the anterior spinal arteries just underneath where we're working and that's a very important structure we have to be very careful of during the surgeries not to

have a stroke or to have the interspinal artery go into spasm because then the patient can wake up with paralysis and so during the surgery we also take breaks where we're just irrigating and checking the neuromodeling signals making sure the blood pressure is at a certain level the mean arterial pressure we like to have at a certain level for these surgeries so that there's careful

observation and maintaining a certain perfusion pressure for the spinal cord and you can see here we're carefully we have continued to debulk the tumor and now we're getting towards the other side of the dura and we're going in and carefully removing the rest of the tumor and you can see its tumor is so large it's going all the way to the other side of the spinal cord and
As we take more and more of the tumor and debulk it, you can actually see the anterior spinal artery, which is right underneath there.
As the tumor starts to go away, you start to see more pulsations of the spinal cord.
And because of the fact that we've removed so much spinal fluid, you're not able to see any of the clear fluid that was there before, but the

The human body produces three times the normal amount of fluid that's in the space, so typically in a day it ends up being about half a liter of spinal fluid and it's recycled, but about 150 cc's of it lives in your thoracic, lumbar, and cervical spine.

You can see here there's still quite a bit of tumor left and I'm just very slowly and meticulously removing the rest of it and trying to carefully go all the way across without injuring the cord and you can see there's the anterior spinal artery.

Again we're staying very far away from it and now I'm getting almost to the edge of the tumor and you can see there's the dura and you can see the attachment of the tumor and I'm trying to cut the tail off the tumor and
This is one of the more difficult parts of the surgery is trying to get the tail off the anterior part of the dura.
So this part of the procedure is important because you want to try to get as much of the tumor as possible and not have this patient get a recurrence.
It's a balance between
preserving neurologic function and taking as much tumor as possible.

There's the anterior spinal artery next to my pen field number four and you can see we're trying to take that last piece of tumor and this is always the most difficult part because it's stuck and it's going all the way across the anterior epidural space and we're just slowly and meticulously trying to remove it and you can see that's the
last portion, and that's the tail that's attached to the dura.
And you can see how the spinal cord at this level is so deformed that even with removal of the tumor, you can see there's a big dent in the cord.
And that just takes time for those tissues and that neural tissue to go back into its normal position.
Now what we're doing is we know most of the tumor is gone.
We're trying to get hemostasis.
Because this tumor grows off the arachnoid villi and the dura, we've tried to obtain hemostasis.
And now prior to closing, we filled the cavity with physiologic saline.
And now we're going to go ahead and close the dura.
We're doing some intraoperative neuromonitoring potentials.
And we can see that the signals have actually improved.
So now when they stimulate the motor cortex, the patient's motor evoked potentials are now more visible on the neuromonitoring.
And you can see that just by taking a tumor out, there's more neurologic activity going in the legs.
So that's how we know that one, that the surgery is successful and it's going to help her without waking her up.
But we also know more importantly that we haven't damaged anything in the spinal cord.
So as a surgeon, you know, I can know with pretty good certainty that this patient is going to wake up with minimal or little deficit.
Sometimes they do have a little weakness just because the amount of, you can get a little bit of edema in the spinal cord.
Now we're using the same exact suture which is a non-absorbable silk suture that's used to close the dura.
The dura is then closed in a watertight fashion and we use a running locking suture so each time we go in we lock it and pull it tight.
This allows us to obtain a watertight closure so that that way there's no spinal fluid leaking.
And once this is done, we then take the remaining portion of the rest of the tack up sutures.
And you can see here, this is now closed in a very meticulous fashion.
And under the operating microscope, we then go ahead and tie the
distal end off.
And once this is done, we then go ahead and place one more stitch and then lock it to the final part, the distal end of the running suture.
And then the distal end is then carefully cut and tightened.
And then hemostasis is obtained.
And tying this suture involves placing actually it's a total of three ties.
And then we put some hemostatic agents into the space.
And then we also put a piece of Surgisil on top, which will enable the Dura-It to heal.
and then we irrigate the wound with normal saline.
And then again, the incision is closed in multiple layers.
First, the muscle is re-approximated, then the fascia, and then the patient is then taken to recovery.
The surgery itself took approximately about four to five hours.
